content: by
Twana Benoit
Original Songs
“Free Ain’t Truly Free” and “I Can’t Leave My
Child Behind”
Written By: Neil Alfred
CAST OF CHARACTERS
The following doubling is intended by the author,
connecting the historical and current characters across
both acts.
— Doubling —
MAMA EUNICE / MAE
Grandmother and keeper of the stories (current) /
Emma’s mother, an enslaved woman who prayed nightly
for freedom (historical).
SAMUEL / JOHN
Alex and Matthew’s father, steady and thoughtful
(current) / An enslaved man, fierce and righteous,
Aunt Sissy’s nephew (historical).
MATTHEW / ISAAC
Alex’s older brother, skeptical and politically sharp
(current) / An enslaved man, quiet and strong, in
love with Belle (historical).
ALEXANDRA / ESTHER
A young woman, Juneteenth speech contest winner
(current) / An enslaved woman, Belle’s closest friend
and “sister” (historical).
ANTOINETTE / BELLE
Alex’s best friend, beautiful and direct (current) /
An enslaved woman caring for her deceased sister’s
child, Lilly (historical).
ANNIE WILL / AUNT SISSY
Alex and Matthew’s mother, wise and grounded
(current) / The head cook in the big house, fearful
of freedom (historical).
REV. MARSHALL, THEODIS / ASHMAY
The family’s pastor, charming and musical (current) /
An elder and respected leader among the enslaved
community (historical).
— Solo Roles —
The narrator. An elderly woman recounting the day of freedom from her childhood. Also called “Peaches.” Appears in both acts.
Young Emma, a child of about seven or eight.
The plantation owner.
The spiritual elder of the enslaved community.
Massa’s young daughter. Peaches’ childhood friend.
Emma’s brother, a boy. Quick-witted and adventurous.
Massa’s son, a boy. Tom’s rival and reluctant friend.
Matthew’s best friend. White. Warm-hearted, persistent, in love with Toni.
Mama Eunice’s lifelong friend. Sharp-tongued, funny.
A young child in the family.

A young child in the family. SETTING Act I takes place on and around a Texas plantation. Act II takes place at the family home of SAMUEL and ANNIE WILL in Lafayette, Louisiana. The playing space is divided between a front yard and a covered porch. TIME Act I: June 19, 1865, and the days following. Act II: Now. A Juneteenth morning. MUSICAL NUMBERS “To Be There Forevermore” — Company “Guide Me, O Thou Great Jehovah” — ASHMAY and Company “Free Ain’t Truly Free” — AUNT SISSY (Original song by Neil Alfred) “You Got a Right to the Tree of Life” — MAMA EUNICE and Company Ring Shout — Full Company “When the War Is Over” — REV. MARSHALL and Company “I Can’t Leave My Child Behind” — BELLE and ISAAC (Original song) “I Ain’t Got Nobody” — REV. MARSHALL “God’s Gonna Separate the Right from the Wrong” — Company
Scene 1
cross to CENTER STAGE RIGHT
(CTR) and EXIT.

1 AT RISE: ACTORS enter from the audience, all LINE 2 go to CENTER STAGE singing, eventually on stage. RIGHT from audience - DOWNSTAGE (DS) of LINE 2, then cross to CENTER STAGE LEFT Actors depicting the characters in AUNT EMMA’s and EXIT. story perform downstage, acting out the story she tells in tandem with her narration. Once QUEEN MOTHER is CENTER STAGE she will fall. ESTHER is the only one to react, 2 AUNT EMMA help her up, then the both EXIT I remember like it was yesterday. Mama woke me UPSTAGE CENTER (USC) into the up, same as usual, saying Peaches! house. All others will continue to walk in line and EXIT CSR or CSL. 3 (MAE enters) Both lines will continually walk at the same pace so that each turn, AUNT EMMA MAE cross, and exit is in sync. Peaches. Come on Peaches— Peaches. Come on Peaches

MAE enters from HL and will stand between the MEZZ. and ORCH. My pa and my brothers had been two hours in the HL (about 3 seats down). MAE field by then and mama had already done a should be doing busy work as hundred chores around our cabin. But her real AUNT EMMA continues -- tying apron, fixing hair, etc. job was in the big house where Massa and his family lived.

DSC And I went too. I even had a lil job myself. I had to gather eggs for all da cookin that went on through the day. 5 Me and mama was makin our way through the quarters up to the big house when this man on the biggest horse I had ever seen went flying by us kicking up all kinds of dirt, nearly blinded me! But mama jes pulled me along saying—
Come on now chile, something’s happening!
What is it Mama? Do you think today is the day?
I sho hope so chile. But whatever it is, we gon miss it lessen you move yourself along!

6 By that time the sun was jes up and I saw my pa and the other slaves in from the field and I knew this had to be something special. I always heard my mama praying every nite since I was born telling Jesus over and over again how we needs to be free.
7 Now Father, please hear the cries of your people. God, you brought us out of darkness and into Your marvelous light. You did not bring us
(center stage). JOHN meet them.
ENTER SL. this far to leave us. Free us from this bondage like you did the children of Israel. You

Now don’t get me wrong we was thankful for what we had, but it was hard times being a slave. 8 Well, me and mama eased up side pa ’nem and she started to ask him what was wrong, but my pa had a strange look on his face. He looked like he didn’t know if to laugh or to cry. He told my mama Massa called for them out the fields. Say there was gon be a union soldier coming to tell us some very important news and we needed to hear what he had to say. 9 My brother, Tom, who was always somewhere he didn’t have no business, ran and told us he had seen a sign on the church and a white man told 10 him it said we was free! Now, brother Tom was known to spit out a lie or two when he had a mind, so ain’t nobody believed him. He even said he saw a whole lot of black soldiers and some of em was on they way to Massa 11 plantation ’long with some white soldiers. Well that helped change our minds about Tom cause now we was seeing em for ourselves! 12 Yonder they came. All in my born days, I ain’t never seen tell of no black folk army. Musta

SR: JOSH, AHYRIS, ISAAC, TOM, SINGER, ASHMAY (Cont.) been bout ’leven hundred thousand of ’em, I SL: MAE, PEACHES, JOHN, ain’t know cause I couldn’t count that high. QUEEN MOTHER, ESTHER, TEKA 13 All kinds of black men. They had guns and everything. One of em, told us they was members Once the soldier is on the porch, of the United States Colored Troops. Tom was so everyone get into the tight clump proud! My brother would’ve run off with em if he again. could, but first, we had to hear what they come ENTER from house with glass. 14 Now by this time, that soldier had jumped down Stand on porch, SL of "soldier" offa that big ole horse and pulled out a piece of paper like a scroll, all rolled up. 15 Aunt Sissy, the main cook in the big house had brung him a tall glass of water. He took a long drank and started reading some words I’d never heard before.
(Offstage)

“The people of Texas are informed that, in accordance with a Proclamation from the Executive of the United States, all slaves are free. This involves an absolute equality of personal rights and rights of property between former masters and slaves, and the connection heretofore existing between them becomes that between employer and hired labor. The freedmen are advised to remain quietly at their present homes and work for wages. They are informed that they will not be allowed to collect at military posts and that they will not be supported in idleness either there or elsewhere.”

16 Back then, I couldn’t tell you exactly what he said, but from the look on my mama’s face, I knew her prayers musta been answered. And my pa, I ain’t never seen him grin so big before in my whole little life. He picked my mama up and swung her clean off the ground, skirts flying ever which way.
(Laughing.)

He knowed how I was lookin, I had to have a turn too! (JOHN twirls PEACHES around. MASSA pushes his way through the crowd to get to his porch.) After we calmed down a bit, Massa went up on the porch like he always do when he got something to say.

17 Alright, y’all heard the man. Y’all’s free now. But you got some choices. I know some of y’all gon run off fast as you can. However, it would be in your best interest to stay on round here. Help us get this crop in. As I see it, you ain’t got no where to go no how. Now, I’ll pay you a decent wage. You can stay in the same quarters as befo. Gon now. Talk it over and I expect y’all that decide to stay will get back in that field fo day in the morning, same as usual. The rest of you, get off my land!
(MASSA exits into house. AUNT SISSY follows)
Scene 2
SETTING: The Brush Arbor (or Hush Harbor)—a clearing deep in the woods, away from the big house. A place of worship and community.
AT RISE: The enslaved community gathers. ASHMAY
stands at the center. JOHN, ISAAC, QUEEN MOTHER, ESTHER, and others are present.

18 Just thinkin bout that time give me the chills. But like everybody else, I could tell my mama and daddy was too excited to think! Good thing, we slaves had learned to depend on each other. So, same as usual, we headed for the brush harbor to talk out what we gon do. Now the brush harbor was a place down in the woods where we could be to ourselves, away from the watchful eye of Massa. It was also our church meeting place, where the folks jumped the broom, where we brought them thats been whipped to Queen Mother for healing and where we buried our dead. Brother Ashmay was a leader of sorts. He was respected by everybody and was usually the one to help us keep in the right frame of mind, you know, focused and encouraged. Mama said he was kinda like everybody daddy. He was one of the first ones Massa brought here. He helped everybody learn the language so we can talk to each other. He kept us from running off when it 19 wasn’t safe and kept Massa from whupping us many a time.

Alright, we done heard the news now, all we gotta do is decide what we gon do. Massa say he give us all the time we want, long as we keep working til we go. Now, y’all know Massa befo today. He ain’t what you call a easy man to work for, but that soldier say he got to pay and Massa said he would.
Why he gon pay for something he been getting for free? And it ain’t like Massa known for telling the truth.
(Others comment and nod in agreement.)

Alright, alright. Settle down. Everybody can have they say. We can all sing together, but we can’t talk together. Listen, I know we been waiting for this day. We bout to have a chance to get off this land. To have a life of freedom we been praying for ever since we got here. We been helping each other all this time and this a’int the time to stop now. So let’s make some plans. If he say he gon pay, only right to give him a chance to do the right by us. Those of you that’s ready to leave, I wish you Godspeed. The rest of us gon get our thoughts together and see what’s gon be best for our families. Alright. Brother John, what you think?
Well, first off, I thank the Lord for His goodness, His greatness and His faithfulness. Hallelujah! My God, we’s free!
(Others join in praises, then quiet.)

Second off, I ain’t willin to work not one more minute for Massa! Feel like my whole life already been stole. Almost ain’t got no mo life left. But my prayers been answered and that’s all that matters. I’s ready to go.
I know, I know. We all feels the same, but we got to be smart bout this. We gots to leave in a way where we ain’t gots to ever look back. And we ain’t leaving nobody behind that want to go.
(Voice calm, resigned)
He’s right. We gotta be safe. These soldiers ain’t gon be here but for so long. Some of y’all need help, and we’s all gon help each other.
(Talks slowly, hesitantly)
It’s gon take me some time befo’ I’s ready to leave. Got to see how my woman—
(Others snickering, nudging each other.)
I means to say my uh, see how my family got to do to get right, you know. Meanwhile, I can help with packing and loading, Brother Ashmay.
I know it’s gon take some talkin to convince Aunt Sissy to leave, but I plan to put a fire under her! So I got time to help too.

I’m thankful for y’all listenin’. But before we do anything else, we gon take a minute to thank our God. Come this way Queen Mother. We got a lot to be thankful for. The Lord promised and He done delivered. Hallelujah!
Song: “Guide me, O Thou great Jehovah”

CALL/RESPONSE GUIDE ME, O THOU GREAT JEHOVAH PILGRIM THROUGH THIS BARREN LAND; CALL/RESPONSE I AM WEAK, BUT THOU ART MIGHTY; HOLD ME WITH THY POW’RFUL HAND; CALL BREAD OF HEAVEN, BREAD OF HEAVEN, FEED ME TILL I WANT NO MORE, (ASHMAY and others continue to hum underscoring QUEEN MOTHER’s prayer.)

Father. God. Our greatest beginning. Our greatest ending. You begun us in our motherland Africa, the home of our mothers, our fathers, our ancestors long ago. You begun us then and You never left us. You never left us whiles we was in the bowels of slave ships, rocking and sliding, dying and birthing, diseased and dis-eased, You were there. You been with us on this plantation; in Massa house, cooking and cleaning, shuttin up and neva sitting down. You, Father, been with us out here in them slave quarters with just a quarter of what we need. A quarter of the food we needed, but You stretched the crumbs in our pockets from Massa’s kitchen and fed us. A quarter of the water we needed but You sent the rain so us could drank our fill. Father, You been with us in a land we ain’t never seen or heard of befo’, looking at peoples we never seen befo’. Didn’t know they speech, they food, didn’t know nuthin, Lord, but You taught us. Taught us what they animals was, how to train them to do what we want. You taught us what they fishes was, and even they lil birds. Lord, You taught us what to do wit em. How to make em taste good.

Father, You know we had to leave most everything, but You gave some of us fo’sight to put a few seeds in our hair, so us could grow our own plants for food and make our own medicines for healing. We done lost so many Father. We done lost husbands and wives. God, we lost chirren and lil babies. Lost to white man disease, lost to selling and stealing and runnin, lost to gambling and whuppins, lynchings, and burnings. 20 But Father, now we here. We here with our bodies, tired, torn, raped, battered and bruised but we here. We here with our families, torn apart, and re-united just to be torn apart again, but we here. We ain’t made it jes yet but we here. We come too far to look back, so we gon leave from here and move forward. Naw, we don’t know what’s all out there in this mean old world, but it can’t be no worst than what You done brung us from. We ready. We following You! We trusting in You! And we say thank You. We say thank You. I say, we say thank You. Thank You for Freedom! Won’t ya help me praise Him? He’s worthy of our praise! Thank You Jesus! I say Thank You Jesus! Amen and Amen. (All are clapping, praising, hugging. ISAAC, 21 ASHMAY, ESTHER, and QUEEN MOTHER exit. AUNT SISSY approaches JOHN, MAE, and PEACHES as others exit.)
Scene 3
22 Ion know what y’all so happy bout. Whe we gon go? Us don’t know nothing bout how to live in this world. Everythang we got Massa give us.
Now Auntie, what you mean? That man say we’s free now.
Free?! What that free? John, you been here since fo you was born, what you know bout free?
23 I know it belongs to me. I know I shoulda had it long time ago; my po daddy, who Massa beat to death, and my mama who heart broke and died too, they shoulda had it!
Aunt Sissy, you ain’t mad we free is ya?

Ain’t mad, jest confused. I don’t know why y’all can’t see your way to stay put for a while. You know Massa corn come in pretty soon and we can make us some nice husk pallets to lay on every night. You know, whiles we traveling.
24 Now Aunt Sissy, you just told Peaches the other day how you never want her to be dragging round
working in somebody kitchen. She can do whatever she want if she free.
25 I was just talking. I figure that baby gon take my place in the big house one day. It’s better than the field anyhow.

26 Ain’t no child of mine gon ever work in no white man kitchen. We all shoulda been done gone from here. Them soldiers here to make sho. Freedom for us. Us was born to it! Now, you is right, Aunt Sissy. That freedom. Ion know what it is, I don’t. But when they tole it, my heart jump in my chest like it was waitin all this time to hear dem words. Like I ain’t really been alive til now. I heard it, now I know for a fact, I am alive!
Aunt Sissy, I know you scared, we scared too but we don’t belongs here, scratching out a living for Massa and his chirren, we got chilun of our own and they need to know what freedom taste like—
Taste?! I tell you what it taste like. Taste like empty belly and dry mouth, that’s what it taste like. Y’all know these white folks ain’t
gon let it happen. They don’t let nuthin good happen to us so it must be a lie.
Auntie, you may as well make up your mind to go cause I ain’t leaving you here. Yeah, we might die getting away from here. But I swear fo God, as a man, and as His chile we going.
(Changes her tone and tactic.)
27 Son, you got your family, just leave me be.
28 No’m, no’m. You took me and my brothers to raise when we ain’t had nobody, and I promised my Uncle Jake, when I was jes 12 year that I would stay round to help you.
29 Well, hep me by staying here. I got all my things here. Yo uncle buried on this ground, right over there; I can’t leave him. Don’t make me leave him, John!
But Aunt Sissy, uncle never seen this day coming. If he had, he’d be the first one on the road!
You might be right chile, but I jes don’t believe Missy Sally and Massa gon let me go. We
ain’t got no papers or nuthin’ saying we free, might as well be running. I don’t believe in taking a chance on nothing. And I don’t believe free is truly free if it mean leaving all we know.

But, you got to believe in me, Auntie this family gon’ make it. Been waitin too long. God done delivered, we got to make it. 30 (They embrace. JOHN and MAE exit. PEACHES stays behind with AUNT SISSY, who doesn’t notice her at first.)
Song: “Free Ain’t Truly Free”

VERSE YOU MAY SAY I AM FREE, BUT WHERE DOES THIS LEAVE ME? I AIN’T GOT NO PAPERS, AND NO PLACE TO BE. THEY SAY I CAN GO, BUT I CAN’T AFFORD TO FLEE, SO I GUESS I’LL STAY RIGHT HERE BENEATH THIS SAME OLD TREE. CHORUS FREE AIN’T TRULY FREE, IT’S ALL A DREAM, IF YOU TELL ME FREE AIN’T TRULY FREE, I’LL BE KIDDING MYSELF, IF I TRY TO LEAVE.
Scene 4
32 (CAROLINE gestures to go play with PEACHES. AUNT SISSY takes PEACHES’ apron and basket, then goes into the house.)

33 Well, I was just a chile but a part of me was sad too; just for another reason. You see, my best little friend in the whole world was Massa’s daughter, Miss Caroline. I loved that little girl like she was my own kin. We didn’t know no better, growing up together. You see my mama worked in the big house in the kitchen. Cleaning and helping Aunt Sissy with just about everything. So, she would brang me and take care of me and Miss Caroline right long side of each other. Shoot, sometimes Miss Caroline would call mama “mama.” Course now, I ain’t never made that mistake with Miss Caroline mama. No, Lord Jesus. I’d never, ever call Missy Sally, mama. Nuh-uh never catch me doing nuthin call for no whupping. I remember what we said to each other when it was time to leave.
Emma, I’m gonna miss you so much when you go away.
I been thinking about that a lot Miss Caroline. I’m gonna miss you too.
Well, I don’t understand why y’all have to go. My papa says y’all can stay as long as you want to.
Miss Caroline—
(PEACHES looks, around then whispers)
—can I tell you a secret?
Why, sho! You know you my best friend. We tell each other everything.
Well, I’m not supposed to say, but I heard my mama telling my pa, that we leaving cause now
(whispers) we’s free!
(Loudly)
Y’all free? Free from what–
Shhhh!
(Softens volume)
Y’all ain’t been in jail round here, y’all live right here with us.
I know, but she say something else too.
What she say, Emma?

She say, when we get to where we going, ain’t no white man gon be able to tell my pa what to do no more. He gon work for us and hisself. And we gon be a real family and have us a house and garden just for us and everything!
...That sound like a lot of work to me.
Same as now, I guess.
Do you think we’ll ever see each other again?
I don’t know. But I’ll ask my mama to help me pray to Jesus every night til we do. 34 (They embrace and exit together holding hands.)
Scene 5

Well, little chirren sho know how to love. It’s a lot they could teach us old ones, I tell you 35 that. On the other hand, them little boys wasn’t gon 36 ever get along. One was always trying to better the other one.
I hear that soldier talk bout how y’alls supposed to be free, and what not.
Yep, me and my daddy been packing up getting ready to go. And I for one can’t wait to leave this place.
Ion know why y’all leaving in such a hurry. My daddy say y’all don’t even know where to go.
37 They got plenty land in this country. I heard of all kind of places for us to be off yo daddy’s place.
Sheriff a’int gon let y’all stay in town. They don’t allow slaves, free or not, to sleep there so—
And we a’int got to go to town, we’ll get our own place and build us a house that’s better than that shed yo daddy gave us to live in.
With what money? You got to have money to live in this world. My daddy got plenty of money to pay y’all to stay here.
38 My daddy gon get his own money. And we gon hunt and fish and my mama can feed us off the land. So we’s ready to leave.
My daddy say it’s a dang fool thang to want to leave when you got everythang you need right here. So y’all may as well settle down.
But we free now just like that soldier say. We a’int got to settle for nothing no more.
39 Well, we’ll just have to see about that.
So you don’t believe it, huh? Them soldiers says we’s free! And ain’t nobody can keep us from it.
Well, them soldiers don’t live round here, they can’t tell nobody nothin! Y’all ain’t free til my Daddy says y’all is.
That so?
40 I says it’s so. Besides, if you was to leave, who I’mma beat in marbles, and racing, and swimming?
Now Mr. Archie, everybody knows you bout the sorriest one round here. I lets you win so you can feel good about yourself.
You do no sucha thing!
Swear fo God, I do. When I leave, you ain’t gon even be able to beat your own self. Even tho you cheats mighty bad.
That so?
I says it’s so.
Well, we’ll see about that. Race you!
See there, always cheating. I can catch him with my eyes closed. 41 (TOM runs after ARCHIE. They exit.)
Scene 6
42 Well, now that was that. I imagine that’s how Mr. Archie and Tom showed they was gon miss each other. All that fighting, but you couldn’t keep ’em away from each other.

43 But love was a tricky thang for the grown folks back in that day. Most times it was the most beautifulest thing in the world. Cause even though you was a slave, having somebody to love you was a good feeling; good enough to take your mind off all the evil that was being done to you. Now Esther and Belle was as close to sisters as you could get. They helped each other through the dark times, and gave each other courage to keep walkin toward the light. Ain’t nothing like a sister-friend. 44 Sometimes, though, with the grown folks, love was the cause of confusion, doubt, fear, regret, even death. 45 (ESTHER enters)
Belle? Belle? You heard?
Yes, I heard. Is it really true?
It better be. My husband already packed our stuff. I’m in there trying to cook, he took the pot right off the fire!
He did what? Wait, were you boiling water again?
Yep! Every good meal starts with hot water!
(Both laughing.)
(Somber)
Esther, y’all really got your freedom?
We really got our freedom! You done forgot the lesson you taught me on per-nouns?
Pronouns.
Anyway! Why you think we was holding all them watch night services? Hoping and praying every new year we would get our freedom. You remember when that seer…what they call him?
Ezekiel.
Yeah, daddy Zeke, say he seen green fields all ’round my Jerome. Say that mean my man gon see freedom. Now that day done come Belle!
Esther, I’m so happy for y’all, I know just what to do.
(BELLE takes money out of the hem of her skirt.)
Girl, what you doing?!!
Esther, you will need money for the journey now, let me help. This is more than I’ll ever get a chance to spend around here, sis. It’s just a little change I saved from selling eggs.

Belle, why you talking crazy? You and Isaac going too! You know that man ain’t gon leave here without you. Besides, the way Missy Sally been eyeing you and Lilly, she be happy-glad to see y’all gone! She always been jealous bout you keeping Lilly. She had Massa sell the rest of his chillun. She might think he gon take up with you.
Missy Sally don’t have a brain in her head if she thinks I want her man, or her sons.

Who would, all three of ’em smell like sour garlic! It ain’t just that, Belle. Nows we free, maybe she thank he gon make you the lady of the big house. Shashying ’round the way you do, using all dem 2 dollar words you done learned from eavesdropping on the whitefolks.
Esther, you stop, I do not.
“Esther, I do not!”
Now you know good and well I only talk like that for the white folks, so they’ll know they ain’t the only ones who can use good English.
We done waited a long time for this Belle. We gon be the ladies of our own house.
I still can’t believe it’s happening. But, Esther...
What is it Belle? Tell me now, I know something’s worrying you.
What if Isaac—what will I do about—Oh, Esther, I can’t even say the words.

You mean, what if Isaac want you to leave Lilly here? Chile look, that meeting they jes had, Isaac, yo man. Yo quiet, don’t talk in front of nobody man. Yo man who ain’t never put mor’n 5 words in a sentence—
Ok, ok, I get it, what he say, Esther?
Well, way I heard it was, Ashmay was doing all the talking like he known to do. Then, Isaac, yo —
Don’t start!
Isaac, say, “Well sir, seeing as my family,…” he say, family, “gon take some time to get ready, well I just as soon use my big ole fine muscles —”
Esther! You play too much! And stop—
(Slapping ESTHER on the arm)
—looking at my man arms. He really say “family”?
Yeah, girl. Shoot, you hit hard. You know that old fool ain’t gon leave Lilly here. And Everybody know you ain’t going no where without her.
He really does love me.
He sho do!
I’ll be a married woman!

Yep, you sho will...Now listen. I sho do thank you, Belle, for being my friend and my sister all these years. When Mama got sold off and daddy took up with that other gal Massa forced on him, I bout died. Belle, I don’t know what I woulda done without you.
Esther, we’re family. We learned a long time ago that it’s not just blood that makes family, it’s hearts.
(They embrace.)
(Choked up.)
Whew. I gotta go fo Jerome come looking for me. You know how loud he can git. I love you, Belle.
I love you too, Esther. Remember, what we always say...
Family is not just blood, it’s hearts. 46 (They embrace again. ESTHER exits.)
Scene 7
47 (MASSA exits house.)
(BELLE picks up baby from cradle.)

Ouu, I love your Aunt Esther so much. I wish I had her kind of faith. Maybe, that’s all I need, just a little more faith. I know we’re free. I know Isaac loves me, but what about your Daddy? Will he let you go? Will Isaac allow me to take you with us? So many questions, Lilly. I am so scared. But what do you say? Can we do this?
(Using baby voice)
Of course we can Mama Belle. We’re free now! Nobody can stop us from having all the love and freedom God has for us. We can, and we—
(MASSA enters cabin)

Gal, I know you done heard the talk. Most of these nigga’s leaving and I say good riddance. After all I did for em. They wasn’t nobody and less than nothing when I bought em. Some of ’em straight off the ship. I gave ’em a home, food, everythang they need to have a good life. Ungrateful, that’s what they are, the whole lot of ’em. They don’t know nothing about living outside of this plantation. Some of em never even been in town, much less wandering around the country. I don’t know what they thank they gon do without me. Dumb niggas. One thing I do know, gal. If you even thinking bout leaving and trying to take my offspring with you, I’ll kill you and that nigga Isaac right in yo tracks. I know he always had it in for me and my boys. I’ll make him watch us take turns on you.
(Slides barrel of gun down her cheek)
Might even kill that lil nigga gal too. Afterall, I’ll have no use for her if you dead. You have a good evening now, hear. 48 (MASSA exits. BELLE sits frozen, holding LILLY.)
Scene 1

SETTING: The front yard and covered porch of SAMUEL and ANNIE WILL’s home in Lafayette, Louisiana. A Juneteenth morning. 50 AT RISE: ALEX paces in the yard, looking over 51 52 pages of her speech. MAMA EUNICE and JANET sit 53 on the porch. MATTHEW and JEREMY enter the yard.
—thanks to the Great Emancipator, Abraham Lincoln. So, in conclusion, we should stand up and never forget the warriors—
Warriors? I thought you were supposed to be writing a speech about Black History.
My speech is about Black History. Juneteenth specifically. Hey, Jeremy.
Hey, Alex. Congratulations on winning the Juneteenth contest.
Thank you JJ, I wish my other brother was more appreciative of my accomplishments.
Alex, you sitting here talking about warriors. We’ve never had any warriors in America. All the

warriors I know were in Africa, way before we crossed the Atlantic, before Civil Rights, and definitely before Juneteenth. So, I could be “more appreciative of your accomplishments” if you were writing a real speech about true fighters for justice instead of all these weaponless warriors!
Matthew, you’re interrupting me. I’m talking about the Black Warriors who fought for our freedom on this side of the Atlantic.
Yeah, Matt. There were a lot of good freedom fighters in America.
Absolutely! And they were non-violent, too! You can be a warrior without being violent.
I don’t know how. All the warriors I know of used weapons. Let’s see, Xerxes, Shaka Zulu, Bumpy Johnson, Al Capone...Nope, I can’t name a single warrior who didn’t use a weapon.
How about Dr. King? He was the best non-violent warrior history has ever known! Not to mention 54 Fannie Lou Hamer, Bayard Rustin, even John Lewis!
What are y’all talking about?
I’m trying to explain to your son that you don’t necessarily need a weapon to be a warrior.

And I’m trying to explain to your daughter that a warrior without a weapon is a fool headed for the hospital. In this day and age, everybody needs to be strapped. Y’all see what’s happening in these streets.
Matthew, it is possible to be a warrior without a weapon. You fight by knowing your rights and standing up for them. You do it by fighting in the courts and at the polls rather than on the streets.
That’s if we could convince our people that every vote counts. My friends don’t think their voices can be heard even if they do vote.
Now you’re talking. During the last big election right here, voter turnout was sad. I even offered to drive some of my boys to the polls only to find out they weren’t even registered.
That’s why when all else fails, you learn how to fight on your knees for the things you can’t change. I say vote and pray!
But you not hearing me.
I knew you were going to say something about praying. You should put that on a t-shirt, “Vote and Pray”!
(ALEX and JEREMY laugh.)
Y’all go ahead and laugh. Prayer has helped me win many-a battle! You might lose a few fights every now and then, but you never lose the war fighting on your knees!
Amen, brother!
Y’all playin.
Sorry, bro, that’s why I couldn’t have your back. I knew you would lose.
But, Sis—
I also knew he wouldn’t give up.
Can I be honest? I don’t think you’re being realistic with your speech. You’re always

talking about how we need to stand up for our rights and we’re not getting anywhere! As far as I’m concerned, Juneteenth is a good example of a bad idea. All that talk about how Lincoln freed the slaves. For real?!
Matthew, what are you saying? The Emancipation Proclamation was one of the greatest executive orders ever written. To celebrate Juneteenth is to commemorate our freedom.

Alright, Juneteenth is the “celebration” of slaves getting the news of their freedom more than 2 years after it happened. Just like now, African Americans are still a day late and a dollar short. I ain’t trying to hear about no Juneteenth. Come on JJ.
Matthew, what are you saying?
Yeah Matt. How do you think we have the freedoms we have today? It started with President Linc—
Oh, here we go. Lincoln. You know what Lincoln said? He said if he could save the Union without freeing a single slave, he would do it. The man wrote it in a letter for the whole country to read.
Damn.
That’s taken out of context—

—Lincoln didn’t free us because it was right. He did it because he was losing the war and he needed Black men to fight it for him. Black men put on that uniform and won that war. We freed ourselves. Lincoln just signed the paper when he ran out of options.
Matthew—
(To Jeremy.)
Come on man, help me out. You know this stuff.
Nah. Imma sit this one out. But from what I do know, without Juneteenth, we wouldn’t have Black History.
See! We need to talk more about Juneteenth. It’s the only real celebration African Americans have to connect with our past.
It connects us with the past, alright. A commemoration of how we worked for free for 300 years, got kicked off plantations with nothing and nowhere to go, or paid little to nothing to
stay. And to top it all off they didn’t bother to tell over 250,000 of us “we’s free now”! Again, I don’t want to hear about no damn Juneteenth!
Hey! Watch it, boy.
Matthew, I’m not worried about you, you’ve been mad ever since I was born!
I’m not mad, I’m tired. I’m tired of people trying to erase our history before the ink has even dried! I’m tired of Everybody having their foot on our necks!
Matt, I don’t understand how my one little speech can change anything.

That’s just it sis. Your one little speech can change one person’s mind; that person may change 100 people, so on and so on. We can’t think about how our one little vote, one little speech, or one little taking a stand at work or school or wherever makes a difference. We have to use every opportunity to say who we are and let folks know we are no longer willing to wait for what should have been ours from the jump!
(Silence follows)
Wow.
Yeah, wow. Maybe I should let you write my speech.
Maybe you should.
Well, I am proud of both of you for taking a serious interest in the future of our people.

I’m not trying to make y’all proud, Mama. I’m just ready to see all Black people become more interested in the future of all of us. We must come together as a people prepared to stand up against these “legalized criminals” coming against us. But I feel like I’m wasting my breath.
Like you said, Matt. You may be the one...
You just may be. In the meantime, we need to get ready to celebrate this Juneteenth, today.
Yes ma’am.
(MATTHEW walks into the house.)

That boy. Imani, Jayden, y’all go in the house and ask your brother to get you a snack. I don’t want you eating at the park like you don’t have a mama! 55 (Kids enter the yard, playing and picking at each other.) Stop now, y’all can go finish playing after you eat, slowly. 56 It’s good to see you Jeremy, baby! Are you going to be alright at this celebration?
Yes ma’am!
You may be the only cream in our coffee.
57 I’d love being the cream in your friend Toni’s coffee. Is she coming today?
Toni will be here, but you take it easy on my friend.
Hey, I ain’t got nothing but love for her; she makes my liver quiver and my heart skip a beat.
(All laugh)
But seriously, Jeremy, I don’t want you to feel out of place, or intimidated.
Who? Me? Don’t worry about me. Even if I’m the 58 only white boy there – imma get in where I fit 59 in. I hope some more palm colored people like me show up. We all need to be educated.
(ALEX laughs and pushes JEREMY to the side)
(MATTHEW comes out of the house)
Mama, I’m sure he’ll be fine. If you think about it, we always welcome other cultures and ethnicities at all of our celebrations.
Humm, that’s true. Matt constantly gets the evil eye every time he comes with me to the chicken run in Plum’Nearly.
Where is that?
Y’all never heard of Plum’Nearly? That’s someplace I never want to go again.
You remember Mama Annie. That’s where my people from. Over there on the back side of Church Point?
I thought Church Point was the back side.
That’s why they call it Plum’Nearly; it’s plumb out the city and nearly out the state.
Ok, very funny. Anyway, Ms. Annie, I’m fine. I love your family, and everybody loves me.
Not Toni.
Mama Annie, can you get your “other son”? That hurt, man.
Alright, Matthew. Leave him alone, he’s in love. Let me go see what these two ladies are talking about.
Scene 2
How y’all doing out here ladies? Can I get either of you a cup of coffee or something?
No, thank you Annie Will. Coffee ain’t gon do nothing but having me running for the toilet.
That’s why I’m wearing my extra drawers, the park ain’t no place to have to use it in a hurry!
Girl, you telling the truth.
Y'all are so funny.
Wait til you get to be our age, it won’t be so funny then.
Not funny at all. They talk about golden years, shoot, ain’t nothing golden about being in the 70 plus club.
Tell me about it; it’s more like gold leaf paper. Everything starts tearing up. Knees, shoulders, arms—
The back, toes. Sis, the other day I lost a whole finger nail!
Whattt?!
Sho did, whole thing just fell off; no particular reason.
Wow. y'all are scaring me. And I was looking forward to my golden years.
Baby, my best advice is to enjoy every second you got right now.
60 I agree. You never know. It sure is a shame we lost Aunt Emma.
Chile, my auntie is not lost, she just running things in heaven now.
You are absolutely right. Bossy and sassy at the same time.
I sure do miss her. Aunt Emma had us laughing for days!
My only wish was that she had lived to share the stories of Juneteenth with my youngest two. She loved children.
I don’t know about that. I remember one time she told us about those little white kids who kept stealing her plums off that tree in her yard. You remember that Eunice?

How could I forget, I knew for sure we would all end up in jail that day, but she was cool. She held her temper for a change. She told them to stop and they did but they also told their daddy and he was a mess.
A hot mess! The whole family wasn’t right in the head. Now you’d think, with them living this close to us, they’d be trying to get along, but nooo. Just as racist as if they lived in Hicktown, USA.
Well, what happened?

I don’t remember the details, but when it was all over, Aunt Emma came back on that porch, sat down and was real quiet. And remember Eunice, Rev. Johnson was here running a revival. He came out on the porch and we told him what happened. He looked at Aunt Emma and said, “Sister Emma, you is really saved; God got a special place for you in heaven.”
And she looked up at him, just as serious as she could be, and said—
(Remembering.)

“Well, preacher, I declare if white folks gon be in heaven, I ain't too particular about going.” Janet fell off her chair, right into the rose bushes! (All this interjected with laughter and disbelief from MAMA.)

Woof. We had a good time with that old lady. But, seriously, I don’t know if I could have grown up when she did. The white folks bad now but back then it was horrible. Just the other night I was thinking about how they can be so mean and evil and still go to the same heaven we’re bound for. Then the good Lord spoke to me and said, “Not all of ’em.”
Well, praise the Lord for that!
Amen!
Please tell me y’all remember all those stories she used to tell.
Oh yes, and today is a good day to tell them. Soon as my son gets back; I’ve passed them down to him and he’s telling them to his children.
Aunt Emma would be proud. (MATTHEW watches MAMA EUNICE from across the way.)
(To JEREMY)
She told me that story three times last month. Word for word the same. Except last time she couldn’t remember the preacher’s name.
She seems alright to me.
Yeah. She does today.
Scene 3
61 (TONI enters)
62 Whoa, what’s happening out here? You didn’t tell me there was a pre-game!
Hey Toni! Those old ladies having a time reminiscing and cutting up! I was trying to work on my speech until your friends here showed up.
(Indicating MATT and JEREMY)
(Flirting.)
Hey Matt, what’s up?
All good, Toni.
(Cool.)
Hi, Jeremy.
Antoinette, my dear. I’ve looked forward to this moment all day.
(Warning.)
JJ—
I’m sorry, I just get so, so, so—
(Grabs TONI’s hand, looking at her longingly.)
So, so, so, stop!
(Pulls TONI out of his grasp.)
Jeremy, what will it take for you to give up on me?
Toni, I’ll never give up on you, I really love you. You’re beautiful, inside and out. Your smile makes my heart beat faster. Your personality is brighter than the sun. You make—
I know, I know, I make your liver quiver and your heart skip a beat.
63 It’s true girl. I’ve never felt this way before about anyone—
Scene 4
64 (MATTHEW collects JEREMY while ALEX pulls TONI away.)
Toni, let me introduce you to the ladies. (As the girls walk away, MATTHEW stays behind with JEREMY.)
Ladies, please excuse the interruption to your fun, but I’d like to introduce my best friend, Antoinette Price, everyone calls her Toni. Toni, these ladies are my family, you know my Mama.
Hello, Antoinette. I’m happy you could join us today.
(Hugs)
Thanks Ms. Annie.
This is my grandmother, Mama Eunice. She’s my Daddy’s mama. This is her best friend Miss Janet. She also goes to our church.
Nice to meet you, baby.
Alright, Ms. Toni, nice to meet you.
It’s so nice to meet both of you. I don’t have much family myself so I’m happy Alex always includes me with hers. And I always learn so much about our history with Mr. Sam.
65 Mama, when is Dad getting here?
Annie Will, I was just thinking the same thing. Shouldn’t Samuel be here by now?
Well, I was expecting him around 9 and it’s only 9:30 now, so we have a few minutes. He had to go pickup Ms. Opal Lee from the airport; then he’s taking her straight to the hotel.

66 Ms. Opal made it?!! Aww man, I wish she had come here first. I’d love to talk with her about Juneteenth, have sooo many questions. What’s her favorite part of the celebration? Has she forgiven the mob who burned her house down? How hard was it to walk all those miles for a holiday that many people didn’t know about? How did she feel meeting the president—
—That’s a lot of questions, little sister. Let her catch a breath, alright?
Who is Opal Lee?
(Shocked.)

67 Only the most important, modern figure in the Juneteenth movement. She’s called the Grandmother of Juneteenth. Because of her efforts, Juneteenth has become a federally recognized holiday. She even wrote a book about Juneteenth and now she even has her own Barbie doll and—
I’ve never seen her so excited about anything— I’m a little worried and scared.

I am so proud of your sister—you should be too, Matthew. This is an awesome opportunity. And my little girl is representing all the young people today. 68 When Sam and Rev Marshall get here we can start getting ready to go to Heymann Park. Oh—
(Squinches face, talking fast)
—and, yeah, why don’t you ladies tell us some stories to pass the time.
(Turning to look at MAMA.)
Wait a minute! What you say, Annie Will?
(Laughing.)
You heard what she said. Eunice are you still crazy behind Theodis?
(Visibly upset, nervous.)
Well—who—who invited him?
Your son invited him. I thought you and the reverend were friends.
They friends alright. Friends, enemies, lovers, all that. It’s a shame you didn’t marry that man the first time he asked you. He’s good looking and he can preach too.
I ain’t trying to be nobody’s first or second lady at this stage in my life.

Well I remember before Theodis got saved, he was a pick! And somebody, whose initials are Eunice Mae Williams, was down at the juke joint every Friday night talking about “I Ain’t Got Nobody, and Nobody cares for me.”
Annie Will! Girl—
(A quick, gentle correction.)
Janet.
I mean, yeah...you. I thought we were about to tell these young people some Juneteenth stories.
Awww, just when it gets interesting.

The young people need to know about how and why we celebrate Juneteenth. Especially you Matt. Alex, go tell the babies to come over here, it’s not too early for them to start learning. (Alex goes into the house to get IMANI and JAYDEN.)
69 It’s sho not something they learning in school. Clowns trying to erase the little bit of history we have. Out of the classrooms, out of museums, national parks, everywhere they can reach.

That’s why it is up to us to tell the stories, repeat the stories and tell the stories again. They’ll never reach our hearts. And one thing about it, history cannot be erased; our history is written in blood.
Mama Eunice, you start it off. You have the oldest memories.
(Beat.)
Start what off baby?
You were just about to tell a story. About why we celebrate Juneteenth.
Oh yes, that is important. We have to keep those stories alive. You know, history cannot be 70 erased; our history is written in blood.
I remember when your Aunt Emma would tell us those stories.

71 My Aunt Emma was the real storyteller, and you sho right, she left me with a story or two. Let’s see. Even though she was born in Texas, Aunt Emma spent most of her growing up days right here in Louisiana. She used to tell me about how they used to have the biggest Freedom Day celebrations you ever seen. Here in Louisiana, we held celebrations as early as 1863, some folks called them Emancipation Jubilees. We didn’t call it Juneteenth til after Texas got the message.
Slavery was pure evil and Louisiana had its fair share of it. But this state has a history most people don’t know about.

See, way before the Civil War, going back to when the French and Spanish were running things, there was a whole class of Black folks in New Orleans who were already free. They called them “free people of color.” Some of them were educated, owned property, owned businesses. Some were wealthier than the white folks around them. That was a different time and a different thing
from slavery, but it goes to show, Black folks in Louisiana been building something for a long, long time. But the main thing to remember is that all of us eventually got our freedom.
When did that finally happen? Like everywhere?
Kentucky didn’t free their slaves until six months after Juneteenth. Lincoln’s own birth state. Delaware didn’t ratify the 13th Amendment until 1901. And Kentucky? 1976. Mama, you were already alive.
I said eventually...didn’t I?
And a lot of the freedom celebrations started in churches. Folks were just happy. And one thing about Black folks, you can’t stop us from being happy.

Our joy rose up from the inside and just bubbled out no matter our circumstances. We had to laugh to keep from crying because one thing we knew, God was not through. Even the enslaved knew they still had a long way to go, but nothing and nobody, Matthew, could keep them from it. No matter how long it takes.

I remember when my Mama used to bring me up to a little town called Coushatta for the big church meetings. Red River Holiness Church. They didn’t have a musician so the pastor used to play all the instruments by himself.
What? Is that even possible?
(Laughing)
Oh, yes ma’am, I remember that. We had some church!

What he did was play the guitar with his hands, then he had his harmonica attached to the guitar, close to his mouth, and he played the bass drum with his foot. And had nerve to sing while doing all that! It was amazing!
Black folks know they can come up with some stuff.
We’ve always been at the top for inventions and innovations.
Most of the credit and copyrights were stolen from us, but we know where things came from.
That’s right baby. But far as church was concerned, we had the music, the singing, and the dancing.
So y'all danced in church??
Did we dance? Alex go get my tambourine!
72 Oh Mama, please...
(Walks away hesitantly.)
Baby we danced every Tuesday night, Friday night and all day Sunday in the holiness church, and sometimes even the Baptist would cut loose.
As a matter of fact, dancing in church started from a tradition way over in Africa.
So this church dancing, how does it go? I mean what did y'all do?

Well, first someone would break out in a song. But not just any song. We sang songs that came from the heart. All the years we spent in slavery, every day we had hope for freedom. We didn’t know how or when, but we knew our Father in heaven would set us free. Help me sing y’all.
(Singing.)
“You Got A Right, You Got a Right-”
“You Got A Right to the Tree of Life.”

Then we had what they call a ring shout. Come on y'all, make a circle, we celebrating! (EVERYONE participates in the ring shout. MATTHEW pulls JEREMY in. DADDY enters with REV. MARSHALL, who dances into the yard carrying an African drum and joins in the dancing and singing.)
And the saints would start dancing around, celebrating their freedom—either freedom to come or the freedom they felt right then. Theodis, show ’em how it’s done!
(Singing)
“When the War is Over…”
“God Gonna Give Me Crown!” (Dancing and singing ends. Everyone settles back on the porch, tired and breathless.)
Whoo! My Lord, Deacon Sam, seems like we found the right place. How you ladies and young folks doing?
(Everyone responds, breathless.)
So good to see all of you. Especially you, Sister Eunice.
73 ...Rev. Marshall.
Uh oh. There she go. Now Eunice, I know you can greet the good Reverend better than that.
Aww that’s alright Sis. Janet. She gon warm up to me after while, cause I’m gon wear her down. Don’t y'all let me stop the fun. And I must say, you’re looking well!
You mean for an old widow woman?
No ma’am. You look good, period. If it wasn’t for your beautiful friend here, I might give you a whirl.
DADDY stands behind her You see what I mean, always flirting around, I ain’t got time for that. KIDS sit in yard
(To JANET)
74 I told you she’d warm up.
(All laugh.)
Hey, baby. I was getting a little worried about you.
Here I am sweetie! I see the whole gang is here! I’m missing the party— (IMANI and JAYDEN run to him, hugging his legs. DADDY greets everyone.)
75 Ms. Opal is settled in; she sends her thanks for the fruit and sweets basket, baby. And, Alexandra, she says she can’t wait to hear your speech tomorrow. So what have I missed?
Daddy, we was dancing!
(Incredulous)
And we was singing church songs—at home!
(All laugh.)
Scene 5
You gentlemen are just in time for more storytelling.
76 Come on Rev. Marshall, sit down and help me tell some of these Juneteenth stories. After all that sanging and dancing it was time to eat.
That’s what I’m talking about!
Well, I had a best friend name of Gertie, Gertie Mae Cook. Her mama was the best cook in the whole parish! Every time Freedom Day came, we had the food!
Tell us about the food Mama Eunice. The only thing I love more than Toni is food.
(All laugh. TONI rolls her eyes.)
Mama Annie feeds me all the time, my Moms be trying but can’t nobody live on eggs and rice every day.
Go ’head Rev. Marshall. I believe, besides Eunice, the food was your favorite part too.
Well, since you put it so beautifully, I will.
Yes! I want to know what they ate! Tell me Everything about Juneteenth food!
Don’t mind my brother from another mother. He gets so excited being around my people.

I just identify with the love and soul that’s so richly embedded in the African American culture. The stories, the dancing, and the food. It’s hard to describe; it’s more like a feeling, a passion. Even in spite of all the pain you all endured and injustices you continue to face, this joy you have is incredible, it’s undeniable, it’s– (Suddenly realizing he has the attention of everyone.)
...What?
JJ, I think when God was adding the color He ran out before He could finish you up.
(All laugh.)

Well me and Annie Will did some cooking last night but the celebrations we have now ain’t nothing compared to how it once was. I remember back in the day, my mama ’nem would be cooking all week getting ready for Juneteenth.
Mmhum! Funny thing now is we used to eat every part of the pig–everything the white folks threw out. Now you can find some of that same food in expensive, Michelin Star, restaurants!
Got me thinkin about all that bar-be-que. Bar- be-que chicken, bar-be-que pork, smoked ribs, smoked brisket, short ribs, pigs wasn’t safe nowhere in the county.
Sho wasn’t. We had pigs feet, pig knuckles, ham hocks, pork maw—.
Fried chicken, fried fish, chitlin’s, beef tongue!
Tongue?! Now that’s just nasty!
That was good eating back then!
Girl, that’s good eatin’ now! And how about all that white rice, okra, cabbage, collard and
mustard greens, and the hot water cornbread to go with them.
That’s what I’m talking about!
Got some in the basket for you honey!
Just talking about all this food is making me full.
You ain’t heard the half! How about the corn on the cob, potato salad, and every kind of bean and pea under the sun.
Butter beans, white beans, string beans, snap beans, lima beans, pinto beans, red beans, black eyed peas, crowder peas, purple hull peas—
—And the sweets and treats!
Girl, only time in the year we would get that good ole, homemade, vi’nella ice cream.
Man, my mama would cook those eggs and cream and I had to turn that ice cream maker look like all day!
Okay, I’ve heard it all. y'all cooked ice cream?
And I could always find something to go underneath that ice cream—
—Here he go...
Apple pie, peach pie, chess pie, blueberry pie, cherry pie, lemon pie, mincemeat pie, buttermilk pie, pecan pie, and my personal favorite, Spicy’s Sweet Potato Pie!
SPICY?!
How about the blackberry cobbler, peach cobbler, lemon cake, pound cake, chocolate cake, butter cake, carrot cake, coconut cake, gingerbread, bread pudding—
Shoot! I forgot the banana pudding I was supposed to bring! I made it this morning. I’ve got to go get it.
You can cook too?
(Lays back.)
God is so good!
Matt, can you come drive me?
Sure. JJ, you coming?
Oh no, with all this food talk, I need to be near the kitchen! 77 (MATTHEW and TONI exit.)
y'all forgot to mention the cold watermelon and the red soda water!
Yup! Aunt Emma said it always had to be red.

And it goes way back, further than slavery. In Africa, red means strength, power, the spirit world. Long before we ever saw this side of the Atlantic, our people been drinking, uh— red drinks made from—uh—
Hibiscus flowers.
That’s right!
And that watermelon? That’s an African fruit. Our ancestors carried seeds in their hair so
they could plant them here when they got off the boat.
I will never look at Strawberry Fanta the same way again. But I know y’all had to be drinking something a little more potent than soda.
Oh, we had something else alright. I’m talking about the homebrew the Rev and his brothers used to sneak off in the woods to drink!
We were just trying to get loose for the dancing. Remember this?
Song: “I Ain’t Got Nobody.”

VERSE YES, I AIN’T GOT NOBODY, BABY AND THERE’S NOBODY CARES FOR ME MM, I’M SO SAD AND LONELY, BABY, YEAH WON’T SOMEONE TAKE A CHANCE WITH ME? VERSE I’LL SING MY LOVE SONG, I SING IT ALL THE TIME WON’T SOME OF YOU HOT MAMA’S RUN HERE AND SORTA JUST BE MINE? YES I, I AIN’T GOT NOBODY, BABY, YES BABY, NOBODY, NOBODY CARES FOR ME
Scene 6

REV goes to bench ALEX DADDY on stool This must be the best day ever! I love hearing about all this, even the tongue. ALEX and MAMA on edge of porch DADDY JEREMY on steps We can’t ignore the hard, sad stories too, baby girl. But if you’re in the mind to listen, we can tell ’em.
Daddy, I study a lot of Black history, but it’s not the same as hearing it from real live people.
That’s why I made your dad listen and remember, so he could tell you and your children the stories.

Well there is a story that I’ll never forget. Before I was called to the ministry, me and some of the young folks from church could have died just cause we stopped to fix a shoe heel. You remember that Mother Eunice?
Just like it was yesterday. It was right after church. All the young people was walking home and one of the girls heels fell off her shoe. I guess she was dancing too hard.
Well, we was searching around for the nail so we could fix it. Old man Mouton must of thought we was gathering up to rob his lil store.
And I don’t know why, old nasty store! Never had one civil word for his Black customers.
Anyway, long story short, he called the law on us. Sheriff and a couple of deputies came right out and put us in one cell. Girls and boys alike.
Our Pastor at the time, Rev. Brown, talked the sheriff into letting us go.

I still think about that night sometimes. Sitting in that cell, girls crying, boys trying to act tough. I was scared to death. I think that’s the night God started working on me, even though I wasn’t ready to listen yet.
I can’t believe they put y’all through that for a shoe.
Back then, good men died for less.
Black men and Brown men and women die for even less now.
It is sad that the narrative hasn’t changed much. From before Juneteenth, til when you were growing up and even now.
Do you young folks remember the story Aunt Emma used to tell about Belle and Isaac?
That’s the one they need to hear. That’s the one I promised my Aunt Emma I’d never let die. Let’s see here. Uhh — help me Holy Ghost — Uhm...baby— Samuel. Y’all gon help me.
(To MAMA)
She having more of them days?
We just take it one story at a time.
79 There was a woman. Belle. She was — she was the kind of woman who could walk into a room and the air changed. Esther always said—
(Beat.)
Esther was her sister-friend.

Her sister-friend. That’s right. And when the day came — When Juneteenth came, she had a child that wasn’t hers, and a man who— (BELLE walks into the yard. No lighting shift yet. She’s holding LILLY.)
(MAMA EUNICE sees. Others don’t.)
Isaac had been planning his bags for a long time. His bags were always packed. And Belle— I don’t remember every word anymore. But I remember what it cost.
Scene 7
80 (Lights shift and ISAAC enters)
Belle, did you hear the news? Girl, we waited so long, we done got our freedom! Lord, how I prayed for this day!
Now ain’t nothing to stop us from jumping the broom!
And jumping in the bed!
Isaac!
What? That’s what folks in love do, and I sho love me some Belle!
So, let’s go. Where’s your bag? I had my bags packed for a long time! I need to see if this stuff still fit. Belle, we free now. Do you know what that means?
I sho do. It means we don’t have to do nothing no more for nobody but us. I can be the lady of my own house, ’stead keeping house for Missy Sallie, wit her old mean, hateful self.

It means I can be a man. I ain’t got to hold my eyes down no mo. I can look a man straight in his eyes so he can see I’m a man too. I can take care of my own family and not worry about nobody taking them away from me.
We can be together, real husband and wife; you ain’t got to be scared about sharing me with master or his sons.

Hell, I was scared for them! I feel like God finally heard our cry and He up there laughing too. First thang we gon do is— Belle. What’s wrong? You sad about us being free? (BELLE lays hand on LILLY’s crib, looking at him with forlorn expression)
Isaac, you know I can’t leave Lilly. I promised my dying sister I’d take care of her child.
I know all about your promise. But what about me and the babies we gon have?
But how I’m gon explain to my sister when I get to heaven why I left her baby in the hands of these monsters.
We got our freedom.
(Turning away from BELLE)

Ok. Ok. It’s not like we running. It’s gon be hard starting out just us; having a half white child to boot... I don’t know Belle. The white folks ain’t gon be happy as it is, and when they see Lilly, it’s gon be even mo trouble for us.
(Angrily)
It ain’t right!
That’s why you got to go on without me, Isaac. I got to stay. Lilly is my child now, and I can’t leave her. I won’t.
Oh, no. I ain’t leaving without you, Belle. I ain’t going nowhere without you.
Isaac, I can’t ask you to stay here with me. I can’t and I won’t.
You didn’t ask me nothing. I said we going.
You young and strong and handsome. I seen the way Fannie Mae been looking at you—
Belle! Listen to me girl. I might die if I stay here but I’ll surely die if you don’t come with me.
(Beat. Angry, louder)
It ain’t right we wait this long for freedom and still chained to this place, to these… white devils is what they is. It ain’t right! I gotta figure this out.
Song: “I Can’t Leave My Child Behind”

VERSE 1 (BELLE) ISAAC, HEAR ME WHEN I SAY FREEDOM CALL MY NAME TODAY BUT THERE’S A WEIGHT I CANNOT FLEE LOVE DONE WRAPPED ITSELF ROUND ME VERSE 2 (ISAAC) BELLE, WON’T YOU PLEASE LISTEN TO ME IF WE STAY ON THIS LAND WE’LL NEVER BE FREE YOU’RE EVERYTHING I EVER WANT AND NEED I CAN’T LIVE WITHOUT YOU, I NEED YOU TO SEE REFRAIN (REPEAT 2 TIMES) I CAN’T LEAVE MY CHILD BEHIND I CAN’T LEAVE MY CHILD BEHIND FREEDOM PULLING ON MY FEET BUT MY BABY HOLDING MY MIND VERSE 3 (BELLE) I LOVED YOU QUIET, I LOVED YOU STILL LOVED YOU PAST MY OWN FREE WILL BUT A STRONGER LOVE DONE CAME TO BE A LIFE BORN HERE, DEPENDING ON ME REFRAIN I CAN’T LEAVE MY CHILD BEHIND I CAN’T LEAVE MY CHILD BEHIND IF I WALK AWAY TODAY I LOSE THE BEST OF ME OUTRO (BELLE) IF LOVING MY CHILD IS MY CHAIN THEN I WILL STAY AND BEAR THE PAIN
Scene 8

Seems like my prayers ain’t been answered after all. And it ain’t Lilly fault who her daddy is, po baby. The other day, I was headed back to the big house with Lilly and Missy Sally seen us. She say, “Get that bastard chile out the sun. She already ain’t got no mammy, you want her to be black too?” I cannot leave Lilly with somebody like that. She my own flesh and blood. We’ll be alright if we stay, it sho can’t get no worse. Massa already warned me he’d kill me if I took her, I—
What that you say? Warned you?

Oh never mind, Isaac. It don’t matter. I want you to get away from here. Make a life for yourself. Find you a good wife and make some beautiful babies. I had so many plans for us. Just the other day, I planted seeds for flowers for us jumping the broom. I started stitching on the quilt for our marriage bed. It was just scraps put together from the big house, but it was sho gon be pretty. I’m so sorry Isaac. I’m just not strong enough to leave. My mind wouldn’t be right and my heart would be broken. You deserve a whole woman.
Belle. Belle, we gon take Lilly with us.
Lilly and I will just take care of each other. Fo’ you know it, she’ll be a big girl. My sister will be so proud. We gon—
Belle! I said we gon take Lilly with us.
Isaac, you mean it?
She take you for her mama?
I’m all she got.
Then can’t nobody be her daddy but me.
Isaac, I love you so much.
I love you too, girl. To death.
(They embrace.)
Scene 9

Well, they laid a plan to set out, taking the little baby girl with them. The other slaves had been leaving for days. Some just threw them croaker sacks, and hoes down and took off walking straight out the fields and never looked back. Others, like Belle and Isaac, took longer to plan when and where they were going. Most traveled north to other kinfolk. But these two lovers had to find a time when they could get the baby away safely. Neither one of them would listen to the warnings folk like Aunt Sissy gave them. Their hearts were for each other and the baby and their minds were set on freedom.
Honey, you got everything?
Much as I could bring. I know we had to pack light. Isaac, I’m so scared.
We done made our choice. We got to trust the good lord to take care of us. But really and truly, I’m scared too. But we gon be alright. Let’s go.
Sshh. Hush now, Lilly. Your mama want you to be free. And your Daddy Isaac gon make sure you stay free.

Well, they took off. They already knew that as soon as master found out, somebody would probably be after them. But they had made a choice to start a new life together. Even though they were free, their previous owner still threatened their freedom. Make no mistake, he didn’t plan to do right by the child, he just didn’t want somebody taking her. You see, it was hard for white folks to realize we were no longer property. They had traveled for almost a week without incident and then they ran into some white folks. They just passed by quietly though. Everybody was looking but nobody said nothing. They thought they would be alright but by the next evening, they heard horses behind them…
(Sound of running horses, dim then louder.)
Isaac, you hear that?
I been hearing it. Give me the child. When I say run, you don’t question me, you take off that way. Don’t look behind you, just run hard Belle. You gon come up on a big old hollowed out tree.
Crawl up in there and put a few limbs around you, they will pass right by. I seen it when I was planning to run a while back. Give her to me.
(Reaches for baby)
God spare, Lilly and I will circle back and find you!
But Isaac, what you gon do with a baby by yourself? You don’t know—
Belle, I’ma do what I have to do. I’m her Daddy now and you my wife, you listen to what I say!
(Reluctantly hands him the baby)
Alright, if you sho’. If I get there, I’m gon wait for y'all the rest of my life. You know I wouldn’t ask you to do this. You can make it, if you by yourself.
I can’t make nothing by myself. You can run faster if you don’t have to worry about Lilly. I promise you, she safe with me.
I pray God take care of y'all. Keep looking for me Isaac!

under Go, Belle. Run!! GUNSHOTS 82 (Both take off running. The sound of hoofbeats LIGHTS UP grows even louder. Stage goes black. Three ISAAC enters SL looking for seconds of silence, then one loud gunshot BELLE followed by two more. Smoke and haze.)

CS behind curtian (Whispering) BLACKOUT can’t...see...yo— BELLE exit SR No, no, Belle, wake up. I’m here. Look, Lilly’s here too. We found you. We’s free now Belle. We’s free! No, no, no! Belle, Please come back to me, Belle! Belle!!!
(ISAAC weeps. Lilly starts to cry.)
Scene 10

83 (Offstage) So close to freedom... 84 Isaac held her til the sun came up. Then he wrapped that baby girl close to his chest and kept walking. Walked for days. Wouldn’t stop, wouldn’t rest. Folks along the way tried to help him but he didn’t trust nobody. Just him and Lilly and the road. 85 He finally came to a place where some free Black folks had settled and they took him in. And he raised that child like she was his own. Never married, his heart still belonged to Belle. But Lilly– AUNT EMMA MAMA EUNICE Lilly grew up free. Lily grew up free. And that’s— And that’s—
And that’s what Belle died for.
Did the Massa ever try to find Lilly?
Well, the thing is, Jeremy, Massa didn’t care about Lilly. Didn’t look at her as his child. She was just property, like all the other enslaved people on his land.
Slavery was the most shameful part of history and we’ve had some awful, evil things happen to all kinds of peoples.
I just don’t understand why being Black is still so, so—
Dangerous.
Yeah, dangerous.

How am I supposed to stand up there and talk about freedom when this is what it cost? (REV. MARSHALL is quiet. The conversation continues but he’s somewhere else. MAMA EUNICE notices and puts her hand on his arm. He covers her hand with his. Two old people holding each other up. Then he’s back.)
The real problem is one that continues even today; people treating each other like they are not equal, not human, and don’t belong.
Jeremy no one puts the blame on you, you weren’t there. We are not the mistakes of our past; our responsibility now is to learn from them. 86 (MATTHEW and TONI return to the porch carrying a dish.)
Oh, Lord, y’all must be talking about Isaac and Belle.
That story is so sad, I’m glad I missed it.
(To MAMA EUNICE)
You told it good, Mama. Every word, just like always.
That’s cause I had a good listener.
Every time I even think about that story I get mad all over again. But I know what y'all are going to say. “You must forgive, son.”
You’re absolutely right.
Well, what I want to know is how you tell the difference between forgiving and just being weak?
I know exactly what you mean, son! I’ve been asking myself the same thing since I got grown.
Forgiving is not what most people think. It’s not forgetting a wrong done to you. Who can forget that somebody killed the person you love? I know I couldn’t.
Damn straight!
Who can forget they served years in prison for a crime they didn’t commit simply because of the color of their skin? I know I couldn’t.
Damn straight!
—But forgiveness is asking God to help you treat them like it never happened. It doesn’t make you weak, Matthew, it makes you strong.
And as a Christian, there can be even harder questions. How far is too far? How long is too long? It takes more than just wearing pants every day to be a man.
And every day, somebody or something is testing your manhood. Trying to find out if you are what you look like. Having to prove myself over and over again is wearing me out!
My Lord, Sam! Why are you so angry all of a sudden?
How things never change! It’s still hard for the black man in America. Still being lied to, disregarded, and disrespected.

Things do change, Samuel. I praise God that you, Matthew, and even Theodis never had to endure the kind of disregard and disrespect our ancestors like Isaac had to. There are still some sad days in America. But we have to stay positive. We have to keep the faith!
But sometimes they push you too hard. Everyday. At work, at school, shoot, just trying to live.

Well son, then you push back. Not with your fists, not with a weapon but with education, with determination all the while maintaining your dignity. I know because everytime somebody tries to test me and I’m about to lose my dignity, my mama and my Annie Will are there to remind me.

And when you claim to be a follower of Christ, they test you even more. You won’t believe how many people, who claim to love Jesus, want to tell you how to vote, how to worship, even how to pray. But like my mama taught me long time ago, ain’t nobody God but God! And when He comes, He’s going to set this all right. In that great separation day. He’ll show ’em what segregation really is!! Like that old song says—
(Singing acapella.)
“God’s going to separate the right from the wrong one day.”
(Singing)
“God’s gonna put everybody in their place.”
(Singing)
“Goes for the women as well as the men, I don’t know where and I don’t know when.”
(Singing)
“He’s gonna separate the right from the wrong, one day.”
Ouuu that song kinda cuts.
That’s why we gotta make sure we’re ready.
Regardless of what’s done to us, we have to stay free, in our minds and hearts.
Even though the old methods of slavery and Jim Crow are gone, other kinds of bondage still exist.
If you watch the news even a lil bit, you realize this world is going backwards. But we have to do what we need to to push it forward.

I believe the danger is people not believing God is who He says he is. That He created all of us and regardless of your interpretation of the constitution, His freedom applies to all of us. REV MARSHALL Amen, Mother Eunice!
We have to get away from those racist belief systems that try every which way to hold us down.
I know I have some relatives that still stand on that stuff. Friendships like the one Matthew and I have are real, but most people think we only hanging out cause we’re selling drugs.
I understand, thank God y'all are not. Right?
Oh no ma’am. We would never sell the drugs we have—
What?!
I mean like we don’t have enough to sell—
Oh lord! Dad, Mom no we’d never sell drugs. Period. Jeremy learn how to just answer the question man.

That’s why the stories matter. Long as somebody telling them, they can’t make us forget who we are. They can’t make us forget where we come from and who we are. That’s why theses stories matt— (MAMA EUNICE tries to continue. The words don’t come. A beat. She looks down at her hands. JANET places a hand over hers. REV. MARSHALL moves closer. The family is still. MAMA EUNICE looks up. Aware. She knows what just happened.)
Y'all got to remember for me now. Cause I can’t always find the words.
You got to believe in me, Mama. We got it. Every word. Every story. I promise you.
We should go. They waiting on us at the park.
(A beat. Then, to ALEX.)
And you got a speech to give, little sister. Make sure they hear you.

Alright everybody, let’s get ourselves together. We got a celebration to get to! (The family audibly moves through the yard gathering baskets, food, and children. MAMA EUNICE is the last to rise. REV. MARSHALL offers his hand. She takes it. ALEX remains on the porch. She watches them go. Then she sits, opens her notebook, and reads what she has. She
So, we must stand up and never forget the warriors. (She stops. Crosses that out. Writes. Starts again.)

So, we must stand up and become the warriors. But remember: a warrior without a weapon is a fool headed for the hospital. So what is your weapon? What will you choose to fight with? If you are a drummer, your rhythm is your weapon. If you are a doctor, your research is your weapon. If you are a singer, your voice is your weapon. If you are a writer, use your words. If you are a teacher, it’s your classroom. Since I am Alexandra, my weapon is my community. And once we all decide to use our weapons together, we will be unstoppable. We are scholars, dreamers, believers, caretakers. 87 (AUNT EMMA enters)
We are producers, musicians, authors, poets, playwrights, actors, directors, singers, and dancers.
We’re mothers and fathers, aunties, uncles, sisters, brothers, cousins, nieces, and nephews. We’re family. We’re still here. Still planting. Still carrying the seeds.
We’re holders of the secrets of how to live in this world. And now you know the story too. Don’t let it die.
END OF PLAY
STAGE RIGHT
